one_line_takeaway: Favia shows that a ReAct LLM agent given pre-commit repository access and explicit CVE/CWE tools substantially improves precision (while preserving near-98% recall) over prior single-pass LLM and non-agentic ML baselines for vulnerability-fix-commit identification, but only once evaluation is redesigned (the paper's own CVEVC 'realistic' hard-negative dataset) to stop the randomly-sampled candidate sets used in prior work from inflating F1 by up to 95% and masking the task's true difficulty.
key_finding: On the random_10 setting, Favia achieves the best F1 for every backbone model tested: 0.79 (Llama-3.3-70B), 0.87 (Qwen3-235B-A22B), and 0.72 (gemma-3-27b), versus PatchFinder's 0.36 and VulFixMiner's 0.03 F1. On the realistic (PatchFinder_top10) setting -- the paper's central claim -- Favia again achieves the highest F1 for every model: 0.46 (Llama-3.3-70B, precision 0.30/recall 0.94), 0.56 (Qwen3-235B, precision 0.39/recall 0.98), and 0.37 (gemma-3-27b, precision 0.23/recall 0.98), compared to LLM4VFD's F1 range of 0.30-0.45 and CommitShield's 0.21-0.35 on the same backbones, and to PatchFinder's 0.38 and VulFixMiner's 0.06 as non-agentic baselines. The authors report that moving from random to realistic evaluation drops F1 substantially for all methods and that random sampling inflates F1 by up to 95%, while Favia's relative precision advantage over other LLM-based methods widens under the realistic setting (e.g., precision improved by up to 92% over prior LLM-based approaches per the abstract's framing) because it preserves near-perfect recall (up to 0.98) while cutting false positives. A qualitative case study on CVE-2014-9625 (VLC) shows CommitShield and LLM4VFD both incorrectly labeling commit 705a427 (a stream-initialization error check in vlc_xml_encode) as the patch by over-interpreting generic safety language ('undefined behavior', analogy to an unrelated Kodi fix), whereas Favia correctly rejects it by explicitly comparing the CVE's stated root cause (an integer-truncation-driven buffer overflow in GetUpdateFile, misc/update.c) against the modified function/file and finding no match. The failure-mode analysis (RQ2) finds that over 85% of Favia's agent errors stem from superficial association between code changes and the CVE, or misinterpretation of the CVE's root cause, rather than from limited codebase exploration or restricted tool access.
core_contribution: Favia is a two-stage forensic framework for identifying which commit in a repository is the true vulnerability-fixing patch for a given CVE. Stage 1 uses a conventional ML classifier (the authors use PatchFinder as the ranker) to cheaply narrow millions of repository commits down to a top-k candidate set. Stage 2 instantiates a ReAct-based LLM agent (built on the smolagents library) inside a code environment checked out to the pre-commit repository state; the agent is given tools (CVE report lookup, CWE report lookup, code search, file search, open file, scroll file, checkout, final answer) and must iteratively retrieve the CVE/CWE description, localize the vulnerable code, and explicitly causally align the commit diff with the vulnerability's root cause before emitting an explanation, a 5-point confidence score, and a true/false verdict, capped at 20 reasoning steps. The paper's second major contribution is CVEVC, a new large-scale dataset (8,283,424 commits after filtering, from 3,708 real-world repositories tied to CVEs) built specifically to support a 'realistic' evaluation setting -- all repository commits as candidates -- rather than the randomly-sampled candidate sets the authors show inflate reported performance in prior work.
threat_model: Not an attacker/defender threat model in the traditional sense; the paper addresses a software-supply-chain security operations problem: given a disclosed CVE, an analyst/downstream maintainer must locate the exact commit in a large repository (e.g., Linux kernel, millions of commits) that fixes it, in order to assess exposure, backport, and propagate the patch. The adversarial framing used to motivate the task is the risk of failing to identify true patches at scale (citing Log4Shell and SolarWinds as consequences of poor patch tracking), and the paper explicitly frames its 'realistic' evaluation setting around adversarially-hard negatives: candidate commits that are highly similar to the true patch (same file area, similar language) but do not actually fix the CVE, which random sampling in prior work fails to surface.
limitations: The paper's own stated/implied limitations: (1) the realistic evaluation is gated by PatchFinder's ranking recall@10 (48%), so 776 of 1,857 true patches (only 1,081 retained) never reach the agent stage at all -- Favia's realistic-setting numbers describe performance conditional on the ranker already surfacing the true patch among 10 candidates, not end-to-end pipeline recall against the full commit space; (2) the agent is capped at 20 ReAct steps and is forced to answer if unfinished, which the RQ2 'Ran Out of Budget' failure category shows can truncate otherwise-correct trajectories; (3) LLM4VFD was evaluated with its development-artifacts (DA) component removed for a fair comparison against Favia (which has no equivalent artifact access), which the authors justify via the original paper's ablation showing only a 0.03 max F1 gain from DA, but this is still a modification of the baseline as originally proposed; (4) CommitShield was extended by the authors beyond its original C/C++ scope to 6 languages using Tree-sitter/Joern, a reimplementation choice that could affect the fairness or fidelity of that baseline's reported numbers; (5) agent-based reasoning is computationally more expensive (RQ3 explicitly measures token cost across input/output/embedding), a scalability tension the paper acknowledges directly against repositories with millions of commits like the Linux kernel; (6) recall differences between random and realistic settings are partly an artifact of evaluation conditioning (at most one true positive per CVE in the realistic set vs. potentially multiple unrelated patch commits counted as false negatives in the random set), which the authors flag themselves as complicating a naive comparison across the two settings.

datasets_used: CVEVC (author-constructed): 8,283,424 commits after diff-size filtering, from 3,708 successfully-downloaded real-world repositories (3,820 identified), built from raw data of J. Sun et al. 2024 (17,293 CVEs, 4,682 repos, 23,303 patching commits); ~2 TB of cloned repository data; 80/10/10 train/val/test split at repository level
random_10: up to 10 candidate commits per CVE from the CVEVC test split (all patch commits plus randomly sampled non-patch commits)

PatchFinder_top10 (the 'realistic' dataset): top-10 PatchFinder-ranked commits per CVE from the CVEVC test split; recall@10 = 48% (Figure 3), so only 1,081 of 1,857 true patches are retained for downstream evaluation
quantitative_results: Random_10 F1: Favia 0.79 (Llama-3.3-70B) / 0.87 (Qwen3-235B-A22B) / 0.72 (gemma-3-27b); LLM4VFD 0.75/0.77/0.61; CommitShield 0.56/0.66/0.44; PatchFinder 0.36; VulFixMiner 0.03

Realistic (PatchFinder_top10) F1: Favia 0.46 (Llama-3.3-70B, P0.30/R0.94) / 0.56 (Qwen3-235B, P0.39/R0.98) / 0.37 (gemma-3-27b, P0.23/R0.98); LLM4VFD 0.45/0.45/0.30; CommitShield 0.30/0.35/0.21; PatchFinder P0.40/R0.37/F0.38; VulFixMiner P0.43/R0.03/F0.06
PatchFinder ranker recall@k on CVEVC test split (Figure 3): recall@1=0.15, @5=0.36, @10=0.48, @50=0.64, @100=0.66
Random-vs-realistic inflation: F1 increases by up to 95% under random sampling versus realistic candidate selection
Favia precision improvement over prior LLM-based approaches: up to 92% (realistic setting)
Favia recall in realistic setting: up to 0.98
Failure-mode analysis (RQ2): over 85% of Favia's agent errors are attributed to superficial association between code changes and the CVE, or CVE root-cause misinterpretation
Language composition of CVEVC patching commits: Java 13.7%, PHP 13.6%, C 13.1%, JavaScript 10.0%, Python 4.3%, Go 4.1%, others 41.2%
Diff filtering threshold: retained diffs up to the 95th percentile of diff string length; any diff exceeding 153,993 characters discarded
baselines_compared: VulFixMiner (J. Zhou et al. 2021) -- CodeBERT-based commit-level classifier, reimplemented from the VulCurator codebase with batch size scaled up ~100x (8->800, 32->3200) for the larger CVEVC training set
PatchFinder (K. Li et al. 2024) -- two-phase tf-idf + CodeReviewer semantic re-ranking framework; also used by Favia itself as the stage-1 candidate ranker
LLM4VFD (X. Yang et al. 2025) -- LLM-based framework using commit intent analysis and historical vulnerability fixes; development-artifacts (DA) component excluded for fair comparison
CommitShield (Z. Wu et al. 2025) -- LLM + Joern Code Property Graph framework; originally C/C++-only, extended by the authors via Tree-sitter to also support Python, PHP, Java, and Go
